title: Correcting DFS Traversal Order in a Graph

statement.markdown: Given a graph represented as an adjacency list, implement a Depth-First Search (DFS) traversal function that returns the visited nodes in the correct order (i.e., from the starting node to its neighbors).
statement.inputFormat: An adjacency list representing the graph, where each index corresponds to a node and its value is a list of neighboring nodes.
statement.outputFormat: A list of visited nodes in the correct DFS traversal order.
statement.notes: Note that the starting node is the first node in the adjacency list.

examples.explanation: In this example, the starting node is 0, and its neighbors are 1, 2, and 3.

hints.text: Use a recursive approach to perform the DFS traversal.

editorial.title: Correcting DFS Traversal Order
editorial.intuition: To fix the bug, we need to correctly traverse the graph using DFS. We can do this by recursively visiting each node and its neighbors.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach: use a recursive function to visit each node and its neighbors.
editorial.betterSolution: Use a recursive function to visit each node and its neighbors, but keep track of the visited nodes in the correct order.
editorial.optimalSolution: Use a recursive function to visit each node and its neighbors, and return the visited nodes in the correct order.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correct solution has a time complexity of O(V + E), where V is the number of vertices and E is the number of edges.
editorial.dryRun: To dry-run this problem, you can use a small graph and manually mark the visited nodes.
